metadata.summary: Convert strangers to customers via targeted cold email and LinkedIn: build prospect list, personalize, keep emails short, follow up 4-5 times. Requires deliverability infrastructure.

content.title: Cold Outreach
content.summary: A structured approach for converting strangers into customers via targeted cold email and LinkedIn: build a focused prospect list, personalize using research, keep emails under 5 sentences, and follow up 4–5 times. Deliverability infrastructure (separate domain, SPF/DKIM/DMARC, warm-up) is a prerequisite — without it, emails never reach the inbox. Inbound marketing takes months; cold outreach can produce customers this week.

why.p: Inbound marketing takes months; cold outreach can produce customers this week. Done right it outperforms paid ads on a cost-per-meeting basis at early stage. The mechanism: tight ICP targeting reduces wasted effort, personalization improves open and reply rates, and systematic follow-up captures the 80% of replies that come after the first message.

list.item: Zero or small audience; need customers before inbound scales
Targeting a specific niche (role + industry + company size) where lists are buildable
B2B products or services where individual decision-makers can be identified
Outreach to potential partners, collaborators, or press
B2C consumer products — cold outreach doesn't scale to consumer segments
Regulated industries with anti-spam laws that prohibit unsolicited commercial email (check GDPR, CAN-SPAM, CASL for jurisdiction)
Low-ACV products where the CAC of manual outreach exceeds LTV
When deliverability infrastructure is not set up — sending from primary domain risks blacklisting

section.name: ideal-prospect-criteria
section.p: Sending to unqualified prospects wastes send quota and damages domain reputation. A tight 100-prospect list outperforms a bloated 1,000-prospect list on every metric. High volume with an unproven sequence burns deliverability reputation. Validate messaging at low volume first, then automate scale.

rule.statement: Qualify every prospect on five criteria before adding to the list: Pain (has the problem you solve), Budget (can afford), Authority (can decide), Timing (current priority), Reach (contact info obtainable).
rule.rationale: Sending to unqualified prospects wastes send quota and damages domain reputation. A tight 100-prospect list outperforms a bloated 1,000-prospect list on every metric.

rule.statement: Start with 100–200 highly targeted prospects, not thousands. Scale volume only after validating a sequence that achieves 10%+ reply rate.
rule.rationale: High volume with an unproven sequence burns deliverability reputation. Validate messaging at low volume first, then automate scale.

list.item: LinkedIn Sales Navigator — B2B, specific roles and industries. Filter by company size, title, and geography
Industry lists (Clutch, G2, Product Hunt) — curated niches, often with direct contact context
Job boards (LinkedIn Jobs, Indeed) — companies hiring for a specific role reveal that the function exists and has budget
Trigger events (Crunchbase, news) — recent funding, new CTO hire, product launch create natural outreach hooks
Competitive displacement (BuiltWith, G2 reviews) — competitors' customers who have pain points with current solution

section.name: personalization-levels
section.p: Deep personalization at low ACV produces negative ROI — the research time exceeds the customer value. Batch personalization at high ACV signals you haven't done homework and reduces reply rate by 50%+.

rule.statement: Choose personalization level based on deal size: Batch (30 sec, company name only) for under $500 ACV; Light (2 min, recent news or post) for $500–5K ACV; Deep (10 min, specific pain from research) for $5K+ ACV.
rule.rationale: Deep personalization at low ACV produces negative ROI — the research time exceeds the customer value. Batch personalization at high ACV signals you haven't done homework and reduces reply rate by 50%+.

list.item: LinkedIn profile and recent posts — current priorities, recent achievements, interests
Company blog and news — recent product launches, partnerships, pain points mentioned
Twitter/X activity — opinions, frustrations, topic engagement
Job postings — reveals what's being built, what's broken, what skills are missing
Podcast appearances — deep interview content; quote their exact words back

section.name: email-anatomy
section.p: Longer emails have lower read rates. Prospects decide in 3 seconds whether to read or delete. Every extra sentence is a reason to stop reading.

rule.statement: Keep the entire cold email to 5 sentences or fewer: subject (4–6 words), personalized opening (1–2 sentences), value prop (2–3 sentences), CTA (1 sentence). No company history, no feature lists.
rule.rationale: Longer emails have lower read rates. Prospects decide in 3 seconds whether to read or delete. Every extra sentence is a reason to stop reading.

list.item: "Quick question about [topic]" — curiosity, low commitment
"Idea for [Company]" — specific, implies value
"[Their recent achievement]" — personalization shows research
"[Mutual connection] suggested I reach out" — social proof, warm bridge

example.title: Observation-based opening that shows specific research
example.code: Saw your post about [topic] - the point about [specific detail] resonated.

example.title: Trigger-based opening tied to a recent event
example.code: Congrats on the Series A! With 20 new hires coming, [challenge] becomes real fast.

example.title: Generic opener with no personalization — goes straight to spam mentally
example.code: Hope this email finds you well. I'm reaching out because...

section.name: follow-up-cadence
section.p: Cold outreach data consistently shows that 5+ touchpoints are required for most replies. Single-email campaigns leave the majority of potential responses on the table.

rule.statement: Send a 5-email sequence: initial (Day 0), bump (Day 3), new angle (Day 7), case study (Day 14), break-up (Day 21). Do not stop after the first email — 80% of replies come from follow-ups.
rule.rationale: Cold outreach data consistently shows that 5+ touchpoints are required for most replies. Single-email campaigns leave the majority of potential responses on the table.

example.title: Break-up email (Day 21): signals respect for their time, leaves door open, generates not now but later replies
example.code:
Subject: Re: [Original subject]
I'll assume this isn't a priority right now.
If things change, feel free to reach out. Happy to help when timing is better.
[Name]

template.purpose: Cold email template with subject, personalized opening, value prop, CTA

section.name: deliverability-requirements
section.p: If cold outreach domain gets blacklisted, primary domain email remains unaffected. Missing SPF/DKIM causes 20–40% of emails to land in spam regardless of content quality. High daily volume from a new inbox triggers spam filters. Bounce rate above 3% degrades sender score rapidly and can trigger blacklisting within days.

rule.statement: Never send cold outreach from your primary domain. Use a separate domain (e.g., tryproduct.com vs product.com). Warm it up for 2–4 weeks before sending at scale. Configure SPF, DKIM, and DMARC before first send.
rule.rationale: If cold outreach domain gets blacklisted, primary domain email remains unaffected. Missing SPF/DKIM causes 20–40% of emails to land in spam regardless of content quality.

rule.statement: Send fewer than 50 emails/day per inbox when starting. Increase by 10–20/day each week. Use email verification (NeverBounce, ZeroBounce) to keep bounce rate below 2%.
rule.rationale: High daily volume from a new inbox triggers spam filters. Bounce rate above 3% degrades sender score rapidly and can trigger blacklisting within days.

list.item: Delivery rate: good 95%+, great 99%+. Below 90% means deliverability problem — stop and fix infrastructure before continuing
Open rate: good 40%+, great 60%+. Below 30% means subject lines or sender name need work
Reply rate: good 10%+, great 25%+. Below 5% means personalization or value prop is not landing
Meeting booked rate: good 3%+, great 10%+. The final metric that matters

example.title: SaaS outreach to marketing managers at e-commerce companies
example.note: 500 prospects, personalized on recent company news.
example.code:
List: 500 prospects (LinkedIn Sales Nav) Personalization: Light (company news + pain signal) Sequence: 5 emails over 3 weeks
Results: Open rate: 45% Reply rate: 12% Meeting rate: 3% Customers acquired: 5 ($25K ARR)

example.title: Consulting services outreach to CTOs at Series A startups
example.code:
List: 200 prospects (Crunchbase) Trigger: Funding announcements Personalization: Deep (5 min/email)
Results: Open rate: 60% Reply rate: 25% Meeting rate: 10% Clients acquired: 3 ($15K each = $45K)

antipattern.description: No personalization — sending the same template to everyone.
antipattern.reason: Reads as spam. Personalization is what distinguishes cold outreach from email blasts. Even batch-level personalization (company name, industry) improves open rates by 20–30%.

antipattern.description: Sending from primary domain without warmup.
antipattern.reason: A single spam complaint can blacklist the primary domain, breaking all transactional email (receipts, notifications, support replies).

checklist.check: ICP defined: role, industry, company size, budget tier
100-200 prospects identified from at least two sources
Separate outreach domain registered (not primary business domain)
SPF, DKIM, DMARC records configured for outreach domain
Warmup scheduled for 2-4 weeks before full-volume launch
Email templates drafted: subject line, opening, body formula, CTA
5-email sequence defined with clear differentiation per email
Personalization hooks identified for each prospect (news, trigger, pain)
Sending platform configured (Instantly, Smartlead, or Apollo)
A/B test design: 2-3 subject line variants, 2 copy variants minimum
Sample of 10-20% drafts reviewed by human before sending
Tracking configured: open, reply, click, calendar booking
CRM or spreadsheet for prospect follow-up status
GDPR/CAN-SPAM compliance reviewed for target jurisdictions

section.name: best-practices-summary
section.p: Never send cold email from your primary business domain — use a separate domain (e.g., [yourname]-hq.com) with proper SPF/DKIM/DMARC to protect deliverability of your main domain. Start at 20 emails/day per inbox and ramp by 10/day each week; never jump to 50+/day on a new domain without 3+ weeks of warmup. Write every email as if you are sending it to one person — mass-sounding copy gets low reply rates regardless of personalization tokens. Trigger events (funding round, new hire, product launch, leadership change) multiply reply rates; invest in enrichment to find them per prospect. Track positive reply rate (not just open rate) as the primary KPI — open rate is inflated by Apple Mail Privacy Protection and bot opens. Build a "not interested" suppression list and honor it permanently; re-contacting opted-out prospects is CAN-SPAM/GDPR violation territory.

section.name: agent-gotchas-and-validation
section.p: Agents may generate emails that are too long for the format (greater than 5 sentences); enforce length limits explicitly in the system prompt. Personalization hooks hallucinated from vague trigger_signal data (e.g., "recent activity") produce obviously fake opening lines that destroy credibility — require the agent to flag missing or ambiguous trigger data instead of inventing context. GDPR compliance: agents must include unsubscribe mechanism language in every email draft; this is non-negotiable for EU prospects. Subject line generation is highly task-specific; validate against real A/B data from your niche — what works for SaaS may fail for consulting. Human-in-loop checkpoint: review 10-20% of drafted sequences before loading into sending tool; spot-check for tone, accuracy, and compliance. Agents should not configure sending infrastructure (Instantly campaigns, inbox rotation, warmup settings) — these require understanding of current domain reputation state that agents cannot assess without live data.
